description: Manual editorial overlay for FootyBrain MVP players (Stage 2). Factual roster fields come from generated core later; this file holds app-owned learning content and optional TM sourceId links.

fieldDocs.id: FootyBrain player slug; must match src/data/sampleData.js
fieldDocs.sourceId: Transfermarkt spieler id string, or null if not confidently matched to preview squad
fieldDocs.displayName: User-facing name in browse, profile, and quiz
fieldDocs.quizHints: Ordered hints; least obvious first for quiz mode
fieldDocs.importanceScore: FootyBrain importance score (1–99); not market value
fieldDocs.quizEligible: Include in default MVP quiz and daily pools

players.quickFact: His meditation-style goal celebration became one of the Premier League’s most recognizable rituals.
quizHints: Rose through Molde and Salzburg before a prolific spell in Dortmund yellow.
His father Alf-Inge played in England for Nottingham Forest and Leeds.
Norwegian striker who wears sky blue and often scores with his head.
players.playingStyle: Relentless finisher with explosive runs and ruthless positioning in the box.

players.quickFact: Set the Premier League single-season assists record in 2019–20 — a benchmark for modern playmakers.
quizHints: Belgian international who rebuilt his reputation in the Bundesliga after a brief London spell.
Genk academy product who also wore Wolfsburg green before the Etihad.
Whips crosses from the right half-space — ginger beard and number 17 at City.
players.playingStyle: World-class passer who dictates tempo with vision, whipped crosses, and long-range shooting.
players.notes: Not on Manchester City squad in TM 2025 preview; sourceId left null until roster match confirmed.

players.quickFact: Spain fans nicknamed him “El tractor” for how he shields the back line and never stops running.
quizHints: Spanish pivot who left Villarreal’s yellow submarine for Atlético briefly.
Scored in Istanbul to help his club complete a treble in 2023.
Often wears number 16 in sky blue — Euro 2024 winner with La Roja.
players.playingStyle: Calm pivot who shields the defense, recycles possession, and occasionally arrives late in the box.

players.quickFact: Wears number 47 at City — a tribute to his grandparents’ house in Moss Side.
quizHints: One-club Premier League attacker raised in Stockport, not Spain.
Pep once compared him to a Barcelona academy legend from the 2000s.
England international in sky blue who plays wide or as a number 10.
players.playingStyle: Silky dribbler who links play between the lines with both feet and clever movement.

players.quickFact: Set up a goal with a kick from his own penalty area in a 2018 Manchester derby — a snapshot of the sweeper-keeper era.
quizHints: Brazilian who won the Premier League Golden Glove in his first season at City.
Left Benfica’s Eagles before joining Guardiola’s possession side.
Often plays long-sleeved with striking tattoos — distribution rivals outfielders.
players.playingStyle: Sweeper-keeper with elite distribution and composure under high pressing.
players.notes: Not on Manchester City squad in TM 2025 preview; sourceId left null until roster match confirmed.

players.quickFact: His parents reportedly named him after finding “Bukayo” on a second-hand shopping site.
quizHints: England international who took the decisive spot-kick at Euro 2020’s final.
Hale End graduate who broke through as a left-back before owning the right wing.
Left-footed curls from the right — Arsenal’s number 7 in red and white.
players.playingStyle: Direct winger with elite ball-striking, cut-inside threat, and tireless defensive work rate.

players.quickFact: Made his Norway senior debut at 15 — still the youngest debutant in the men’s national team history.
quizHints: Scandinavian playmaker Real Madrid bought as a 16-year-old prodigy.
Loan spells included Heerenveen, Vitesse, and Real Sociedad before north London.
Arsenal captain who wears the armband for Norway — son of a former footballer.
players.playingStyle: Elegant number 10 who orchestrates attacks with disguised passes and clever pressing triggers.

players.quickFact: Won Ligue 1 Young Player of the Year on loan at Marseille before Arsenal fans finally saw him in red and white.
quizHints: French defender Arsenal signed young but waited years to field in the Premier League.
Loan stops included Nice and Marseille before the Emirates partnership chant.
Forms half of a famous centre-back duo with Gabriel — low fade, number 2.
players.playingStyle: Composed defender who wins duels, carries the ball, and reads through-balls early.

players.quickFact: Was West Ham’s youngest-ever captain before a nine-figure move across London in 2023.
quizHints: England midfielder who wore claret and blue before Arsenal’s number 41.
Could have represented Ireland through grandparents but chose England.
Euro 2024 winner who anchors the Gunners’ midfield shield.
players.playingStyle: Box-to-box midfielder with tackling intensity, progressive carrying, and leadership.

players.quickFact: His “call to heaven” celebration is a tribute to his mother, who helped raise him in São Paulo.
quizHints: Brazilian forward who won Premier League titles in Manchester before north London.
Palmeiras graduate whose given name matches a biblical figure.
Mimics a phone call after scoring — number 9 in Arsenal red.
players.playingStyle: Mobile forward who presses relentlessly, links play, and thrives in wide-to-central rotations.

players.quickFact: Worked in a café during a rough patch after Chelsea — before becoming Liverpool’s defining goal scorer of a generation.
quizHints: African nation whose flag inspires his “Egyptian King” nickname at Anfield.
Left foot on the right wing — the near-post curl is his signature.
Roma’s “Pharaoh” before Merseyside records and Premier League Golden Boots.
players.playingStyle: Left-footed inverted winger who cuts inside for curlers and thrives on counterattacks.

players.quickFact: PFA Player of the Year in 2019 — one of the few defenders to win England’s top individual prize.
quizHints: Dutch defender who wore Celtic hoops and Southampton red-and-white before Anfield.
Southampton sold him for a then-record fee for a defender in 2018.
Wears number 4, leads Liverpool’s high line — recovered from a serious knee injury.
players.playingStyle: Dominant aerial defender with calm distribution and game-reading that organizes the back line.

players.quickFact: Scored a 95th-minute header from a corner in May 2021 — one of only a handful of Premier League goals by a keeper.
quizHints: Brazilian shot-stopper whose brother also keeps professionally in Portugal.
Roma’s number one before Liverpool made him the world’s most expensive keeper at the time.
Famous late winning header against a Midlands club in green and white stripes.
players.playingStyle: Shot-stopper with quick reflexes and calm one-on-one defending in big moments.

players.quickFact: Wears number 66 — a nod to the year his postal district was founded, not a random squad number.
quizHints: Scouse academy graduate from West Derby who never left his boyhood club.
Among the Premier League’s most prolific assist-makers for a defender.
Set-piece delivery from the right touchline — free-kicks and corners for Liverpool.
players.playingStyle: Quarterback full-back who delivers whipped crosses and set-piece mastery over defensive orthodoxy.
players.notes: TM 2025 lists Trent Alexander-Arnold (314353) at Real Madrid; sampleData still Liverpool — no sourceId until teams align.

players.quickFact: Almería bought him from Uruguay for about €4M and sold him to Benfica for roughly eight times that one season later.
quizHints: Uruguayan striker whose hair colour changes as often as debate about his finishing.
Portuguese league top scorer season preceded a big Anfield transfer fee.
Often plays central in a front three beside Egypt’s king on the right wing.
players.playingStyle: Chaotic runner who presses high, attacks channels, and finishes with power over finesse.
players.notes: Not on Liverpool squad in TM 2025 preview; sourceId left null until roster match confirmed.

players.quickFact: Flamengo sold him to Madrid as a teenager — he later scored the decisive goal in a Paris Champions League final.
quizHints: Brazilian left winger who wears the famous number 7 in white at the Bernabéu.
Became a leading voice against repeated racist abuse in Spanish stadiums.
2022 final winner against the club in all-red from Merseyside.
players.playingStyle: Explosive dribbler who terrorizes full-backs, draws fouls, and finishes in clutch moments.

players.quickFact: Became Birmingham City’s youngest-ever first-team player at 16 years and 38 days.
quizHints: Midlands-born England star who left for Germany before crossing to Spain.
Knee-slide celebration copied by teammates after big goals in white.
Wore Dortmund’s number 22 before starring in Madrid’s number 5 shirt.
players.playingStyle: Complete midfielder who arrives in the box, presses aggressively, and leads with charisma.

players.quickFact: Ballon d’Or winner in 2018 — the first player outside Messi or Ronaldo to win it in over a decade.
quizHints: Croatian midfielder who fled war as a child and grew up in a hotel as a refugee.
White Hart Lane years before a decade-plus in Madrid white.
Outside-of-the-foot passes and metronome tempo — number 10 for La Roja’s neighbors.
players.playingStyle: Metronome midfielder with outside-foot passes, press resistance, and legendary stamina.
players.notes: TM 2025 lists Luka Modrić (27992) at AC Milan; sampleData still Real Madrid — no sourceId until teams align.

players.quickFact: Once played right-back in a Clásico to mark Barcelona’s star winger — coaches trusted his engine over his natural role.
quizHints: Uruguayan runner with a bleached-blond look in Madrid white.
Peñarol talent signed young by the club with the most European Cups.
Long-range shot in a 2022 Champions League tie injured Liverpool’s keeper.
players.playingStyle: Engine midfielder with lung-busting runs, long shots, and tactical discipline on the wing or center.

players.quickFact: At roughly 6’7”, he is among the tallest regular starters in elite European football.
quizHints: Belgian keeper who represented Atlético, Chelsea, and Real Madrid.
Genk youth product before La Liga and Premier League spells.
Man of the Match in a Paris Champions League final with late saves.
players.playingStyle: Giant frame keeper who excels one-on-one and commands the penalty area on crosses.

players.quickFact: Five goals in nine minutes vs Wolfsburg in 2015 remains one of the Bundesliga’s most famous individual explosions.
quizHints: Poland’s all-time leading men’s international goalscorer.
Dortmund’s yellow-and-black number 9 before Bayern records and Barça stripes.
Also played volleyball competitively as a teenager before committing to football.
players.playingStyle: Complete number 9 with elite movement, link play, and both-footed finishing.

players.quickFact: Stacked club, national team, and Olympics minutes as a teenager in 2021 — sparking talk about burnout before he turned 19.
quizHints: Canary Islands-born La Masia midfielder with a low center of gravity.
Las Palmas loanee who became Euro 2020 Young Player of the Tournament.
Often paired in midfield with a younger teammate known for fiery tackles.
players.playingStyle: La Masia technician who glides past presses with body feints and progressive passing.

players.quickFact: Made his Spain senior debut at 17 — among the youngest in the men’s team in the modern era.
quizHints: La Masia graduate whose first name is Pablo but everyone uses a four-letter nickname.
Long hair and aggressive challenges mark his style beside Pedri.
Serious knee surgery interrupted his rapid rise in blaugrana stripes.
players.playingStyle: Fiery ball-winner with flair dribbles and aggressive personality on the pitch.

players.quickFact: Left Leeds for Barcelona in 2022 — one of several Brazilians who sharpened their game in England before La Liga.
quizHints: Brazilian winger who wore white at Elland Road before Camp Nou.
Breakout in Portugal at Sporting before France at Rennes.
Direct dribbler often stationed on Barça’s right in a trident attack.
players.playingStyle: Direct Brazilian winger with tricks, cutbacks, and relentless work off the ball.

players.quickFact: Germany’s number-one debate often pits him against Bayern’s long-serving keeper — both rose through Bundesliga rivals.
quizHints: Mönchengladbach “Foals” graduate who joined Barça in 2014.
Sweeper-keeper who starts attacks with short passes under pressure.
Catalan club’s long-term number one while Neuer ruled for Die Mannschaft.
players.playingStyle: Sweeper-keeper with reflex saves and excellent distribution to start buildup.
players.notes: Not on Barcelona squad in TM 2025 preview; sourceId left null until roster match confirmed.

players.quickFact: England’s men’s all-time leading goalscorer — he passed Wayne Rooney’s record during Euro 2024 qualifying.
quizHints: Striker who wore white for a north London club for over a decade before Bavaria.
Drops deep to create — often described as a nine-and-a-half.
2018 World Cup Golden Boot winner in Russia — England captain.
players.playingStyle: Classic number 9 who drops deep to create, finishes with both feet, and dominates aerial duels.

players.quickFact: Chose Germany over England at senior level despite captaining England youth sides and growing up in London.
quizHints: Bayern’s slippery dribbler born in Fulham with Nigerian and German heritage.
Left Chelsea’s academy before starring in red in Munich.
Opponents dub him “Bambi” for how he glides past challenges.
players.playingStyle: Slippery dribbler with close control, nutmegs, and intelligent cuts from the left half-space.

players.quickFact: Pep Guardiola shifted him from Bundesliga right-back to one of Germany’s defining midfield controllers.
quizHints: RB Leipzig and Stuttgart pipeline before Bayern’s red shirt.
Vocal organizer who has worn both 6 and 32 at club level.
Crosses from wide were his weapon before anchoring the middle for Die Mannschaft.
players.playingStyle: Tactical leader who tackles, crosses from wide, and dictates pressing traps.

players.quickFact: Helped redefine the sweeper-keeper role and captained Germany to the 2014 World Cup in Brazil.
quizHints: Schalke blue before over a decade in Bayern red.
Famous for racing out to slide-tackle like an outfielder.
World Cup winner in Rio who often wears a cap after skin treatment.
players.playingStyle: Revolutionary keeper who acts as a libero, commands the box, and organizes the defense.

players.quickFact: His mother won Olympic gold in gymnastics — pundits often cite that background for his balance and body control.
quizHints: German winger who wore sky blue in Manchester before Munich.
Schalke breakout with a famous surname tied to Senegalese football.
Often cuts inside from the left with rapid stepovers — father played for Senegal.
players.playingStyle: Pace merchant who cuts inside with flair, direct dribbling, and inconsistent end product.
players.notes: Not on Bayern Munich squad in TM 2025 preview; sourceId left null until roster match confirmed.

players.quickFact: His motorcycle-throttle celebration nods to riding culture — he is known for languid dribbles that suddenly explode into top speed.
quizHints: Portuguese left winger who wears Rossoneri red and black at San Siro.
Ligue 1 cameo at Lille before Milan made him a Serie A star.
2021–22 Scudetto-winning side’s standout attacker on the left flank.
players.playingStyle: Gangly speedster who glides past defenders with long strides and cool finishing.

players.quickFact: PSG never gave him a Ligue 1 appearance — Lille and Milan turned him into one of Europe’s top shot-stoppers.
quizHints: French keeper born in Cayenne, French Guiana.
Won Ligue 1 at Lille before replacing a Cameroonian international at Milan.
Known for quick feet and playing out from the back in Serie A — number 16.
players.playingStyle: Athletic shot-stopper with quick feet and confident distribution under pressure.

players.quickFact: His bull celebration — hands on hips, charging — became one of Argentina’s most copied goal rituals after 2022.
quizHints: Argentine striker who left Racing’s light blue and white for Nerazzurri black and blue.
World Cup co-top scorer in Qatar alongside his country’s greatest number 10.
Inter captain who leads the line in Serie A — bull celebration after goals.
players.playingStyle: Pressing forward who links play, drops deep, and finishes with power and movement off the shoulder.

players.quickFact: Cagliari sold their homegrown midfielder to Inter — he later ran the engine room of Italy’s Euro 2020 winners.
quizHints: Sardinian-born Italian with trademark blonde-streaked hair.
Small stature, huge lungs — covers every blade at San Siro.
Euro 2020 champion who wears Inter’s number 23 in blue and black.
players.playingStyle: Box-to-box dynamo with relentless energy, line-breaking passes, and emotional leadership.

players.quickFact: Left-footed centre-back in a back three — Atalanta groomed him before Inter made him a Serie A title pillar.
quizHints: Italian defender who plays on the left side of a defensive trio in blue and black.
Bergamo youth product before a big move across Lombardy to San Siro.
Progressive passes and stepping into midfield are his calling cards.
players.playingStyle: Modern defender who steps into midfield, slides tackles, and sprays diagonal passes.

players.quickFact: At 1.83 m he is short for a modern elite keeper — yet ranked among Europe’s best reflex shot-stoppers for years at Gladbach.
quizHints: Swiss international who spent nearly a decade in green and white at Mönchengladbach.
Basel boy before Bundesliga fame and a 2023 move to San Siro.
Replaced a Cameroonian number one when joining Inter’s Serie A title-winning side.
players.playingStyle: Reflex keeper with excellent penalty saving and calm handling in one-on-ones.
